conversation.content:
Reconcile the following columns. Document has 10 pages. Sources are anonymous (A and B).

COLUMNS:

---
Column 1: Add-on Treatment
Definition: What is the name of additional therapy or intervention given in combination with the primary treatment in the clinical trial?. If reported, provide the value; otherwise use 'Not reported'.
  A: value="docetaxel" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="The abstract states: 'We assessed whether concomitant treatment with ADT plus docetaxel would result in longer overall survival than that with ADT alone.'"
  B: value="Docetaxel" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="The study compared ADT alone with ADT plus docetaxel. Docetaxel is the additional therapy."

---
Column 2: COE_RCT_IND_OVERALL_RJ
Definition: What is the overall risk judgment of individual criteria of evidence (COE) in randomized controlled trials (RCT)?.
  A: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="The document is a primary clinical trial report and does not contain an overall risk judgment of individual criteria of evidence in randomized controlled trials (COE_RCT_IND_OVERALL_RJ)."
  B: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="No information found regarding 'COE_RCT_IND_OVERALL_RJ' in the provided pages or through semantic search."

---
Column 3: Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1
Definition: What the type or category of therapeutic agent used in the first experimental arm of the clinical trial?. If reported, provide the value; otherwise use 'Not reported'.
  A: value="Chemotherapy" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="Docetaxel is a chemotherapy agent. The abstract mentions 'Chemohormonal Therapy'."
  B: value="Chemotherapy (Taxane)" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="Docetaxel is a chemotherapy agent, specifically a taxane."

---
Column 4: Control Arm
Definition: What the name of the arm in the clinical trial that does not receive the experimental treatment, serving as a comparison group?. If reported, provide the value; otherwise use 'Not reported'.
  A: value="ADT alone" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="The abstract states: 'We assigned men with metastatic, hormone-sensitive prostate cancer to receive either ADT plus docetaxel... or ADT alone.'"
  B: value="ADT alone" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="The methods section states patients were assigned to 'ADT plus docetaxel ... or ADT alone.'"

---
Column 5: Control Arm - N
Definition: What the total number of participants included in the control arm of the clinical trial?. If reported, provide the value; otherwise use 'Not reported'. If the trial contains multiple control arms, report for all separately
  A: value="393" | page=5 | modality=table | reasoning="Table 1 shows 'ADT Alone (N=393)'."
  B: value="393" | page=8 | modality=table | reasoning="Table 2 on page 8 explicitly states the number of participants in the ADT Alone (Control) arm."

---
Column 6: Median Follow-Up Duration (mo)
Definition: What the median length of time, measured in months, that participants in the clinical trial were followed up or monitored after the start of the trial? If reported, include the value; use 'Not reported' if missing.
  A: value="28.9" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="The abstract states: 'After a median follow-up of 28.9 months...'. Figure 1 caption also states: 'The median duration of follow-up was 28.9 months among all patients (Panel A)'."
  B: value="28.9" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="The results section on page 1 explicitly states the median follow-up duration."

---
Column 7: Number of Arms Included
Definition: What is the total count of experimental treatment arms and control arms included in the clinical trial?. If reported, provide the value; otherwise use 'Not reported'.
  A: value="2" | page=2 | modality=text | reasoning="The study assigned patients to 'ADT plus docetaxel' or 'ADT alone', indicating two arms."
  B: value="2" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="The study design clearly describes two treatment arms: ADT plus docetaxel and ADT alone."

---
Column 8: Primary Endpoint(s)
Definition: What the main outcome(s) or objective(s) that the clinical trial is designed to measure or achieve?. If reported, provide the value; otherwise use 'Not reported'.
  A: value="Overall survival" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="The abstract states: 'The primary objective was to test the hypothesis that the median overall survival would be 33.3% longer...' and 'Overall survival was defined as the time until death from any cause.'"
  B: value="Overall survival" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="The primary objective is explicitly stated as testing the hypothesis related to overall survival on page 1."

---
Column 9: Quality of Life Scale
Definition: What a measure of the participants' overall well-being and satisfaction with their health status, as assessed through a standardized questionnaire or survey, reflecting their perceived quality of life during the clinical trial?.
  A: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="No specific quality of life scale is mentioned in the document."
  B: value="Not reported" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="No mention of a specific Quality of Life Scale in the provided pages or through semantic search."

---
Column 10: Quality of Life reported
Definition: Does the clinical trial report results on the quality of life of participants? Answer Yes or No
  A: value="No" | page=None | modality=text | reasoning="The document reports adverse events and clinical outcomes but does not include any reported results or discussion on the quality of life of participants."
  B: value="No" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="No mention of quality of life results or assessment on the provided pages or through semantic search."

Column 11: Secondary Endpoint(s)

---
Column 12: Total Participants - N
Definition: What are the total number of participants included in the clinical trial? These include only participants who were inlcuded in either the treatment or control arm of the clinical trial. If reported, provide the value; otherwise use 'Not reported'.If multiple comparative trials are mentioned, report for all separately

Definition: What the specific treatment protocol or regimen used in the experimental arms of the clinical trial?. If reported, provide the treament regimen used ; otherwise use 'Not reported'.
  A: value="ADT plus docetaxel (at a dose of 75 mg per square meter of body-surface area every 3 weeks for six cycles)" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="The abstract states: 'We assigned men with metastatic, hormone-sensitive prostate cancer to receive either ADT plus docetaxel (at a dose of 75 mg per square meter of body-surface area every 3 weeks for six cycles) or ADT alone.'"
  B: value="ADT plus docetaxel (75 mg per square meter of body-surface area every 3 weeks for six cycles)" | page=1 | modality=text | reasoning="The methods section on page 1 describes the specific regimen for the combination therapy arm."

Column 15: Treatment Arm(s)

response.formatted_chunks:
[Page 1]
## Page 1

The NEW ENGLAND JOURNAL of MEDICINE



ORIGINAL ARTICLE



Chemohormonal Therapy in Metastatic
Hormone-Sensitive Prostate Cancer
Christopher J. Sweeney, M.B., B.S., Yu-Hui Chen, M.S., M.P.H.,
Michael Carducci, M.D., Glenn Liu, M.D., David F. Jarrard, M.D.,
Mario Eisenberger, M.D., Yu-Ning Wong, M.D., M.S.C.E., Noah Hahn, M.D.,
Manish Kohli, M.D., Matthew M. Cooney, M.D., Robert Dreicer, M.D.,
Nicholas J. Vogelzang, M.D., Joel Picus, M.D., Daniel Shevrin, M.D.,
Maha Hussain, M.B., Ch.B., Jorge A. Garcia, M.D., and Robert S. DiPaola, M.D.



ABSTRACT



BACKGROUND
Androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT) has been the backbone of treatment for metastatic prostate cancer since the 1940s. We assessed whether concomitant treatment with ADT plus docetaxel would result in longer overall survival than that with ADT alone.



METHODS
We assigned men with metastatic, hormone-sensitive prostate cancer to receive either ADT plus docetaxel (at a dose of 75 mg per square meter of body-surface area every 3 weeks for six cycles) or ADT alone. The primary objective was to test the hypothesis that the median overall survival would be 33.3% longer among patients receiving docetaxel added to ADT early during therapy than among patients receiving ADT alone.



RESULTS
A total of 790 patients (median age, 63 years) underwent randomization. After a
median follow-up of 28.9 months, the median overall survival was 13.6 months
longer with ADT plus docetaxel (combination therapy) than with ADT alone (57.6
months vs. 44.0 months; hazard ratio for death in the combination group, 0.61;
95% confidence interval [CI], 0.47 to 0.80; P<0.001). The median time to bio-
chemical, symptomatic, or radiographic progression was 20.2 months in the com-
bination group, as compared with 11.7 months in the ADT-alone group (hazard
ratio, 0.61; 95% CI, 0.51 to 0.72; P<0.001). The rate of a prostate-specific antigen
level of less than 0.2 ng per milliliter at 12 months was 27.7% in the combination
group versus 16.8% in the ADT-alone group (P<0.001). In the combination group,
the rate of grade 3 or 4 febrile neutropenia was 6.2%, the rate of grade 3 or 4
infection with neutropenia was 2.3%, and the rate of grade 3 sensory neuropathy
and of grade 3 motor neuropathy was 0.5%.



CONCLUSIONS
Six cycles of docetaxel at the beginning of ADT for metastatic prostate cancer resulted in significantly longer overall survival than that with ADT alone. (Funded by the National Cancer Institute and others; ClinicalTrials.gov number, NCT00309985.)



From the Department of Medicine (C.J.S.)
and the Department of Biostatistics and
Computational Biology (Y.-H.C.), Dana-
Farber Cancer Institute, Boston; Harvard
Medical School, Boston (C.J.S.); Johns
Hopkins University, Baltimore (M.C., M.E.);
University of Wisconsin Carbone Cancer
Center (G.L., D.F.J.) and School of Medi-
cine and Public Health (D.F.J.), Madison;
Fox Chase Cancer Center, Temple Univer-
sity Health System, Philadelphia (Y.-N.W.);
Indiana University Melvin and Bren Simon
Cancer Center, Indianapolis (N.H.); Mayo
Clinic, Rochester, MN (M.K.); University
Hospitals Case Medical Center, Seidman
Cancer Center (M.M.C.), and Cleveland
Clinic Taussig Cancer Institute (J.A.G.)
— both in Cleveland; University of Virginia
Cancer Center, Charlottesville (R.D.); Com-
prehensive Cancer Centers of Nevada, Las
Vegas (N.J.V.); Siteman Cancer Center,
Washington University School of Medi-
cine, St. Louis (J.P.); NorthShore University
HealthSystem, Evanston, IL (D.S.); Univer-
sity of Michigan Comprehensive Cancer
Center, Ann Arbor (M.H.); and Rutgers
Cancer Institute of New Jersey, New Bruns-
wick (R.S.D.). Address reprint requests to
Dr. Sweeney at christopher_sweeney@dfci
.harvard.edu.



This article was published on August 5,
2015, at NEJM.org.



N Engl J Med 2015;373:737-46.

† Clinical progression was defined by increasing symptoms of bone metastases; progression according to the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors, version 1.0; or clinical deterioration due to cancer according to the investigator's opinion.

* Patients were classified according to the worst grade reported across all body systems. Patients assigned to ADT plus docetaxel were monitored every 3 weeks during the time docetaxel was administered and then every 3 months, whereas patients assigned to the ADT-alone group were seen every 3 months after randomization. Toxic effects in the group that received ADT plus docetaxel were captured at this frequency to ascertain the adverse-event profile of chemotherapy. The adverse-event profile of ADT was assumed to be common to the two groups. The potential risk of ascertainment bias for adverse events and early progression in the ADT-plus-docetaxel group was recognized, but such bias, if it existed, would have favored the ADT-alone group.



ASSIGNED THERAPY ADMINISTERED
AND SUBSEQUENT THERAPY
Approximately 86% of the 390 patients in the
combination group who started the assigned
therapy completed six cycles of docetaxel therapy
(Table S2 in the Supplementary Appendix), and
approximately 74% of all the treated patients
received all planned cycles without dose modifi-
cations. At the time of this analysis, castration-
resistant prostate cancer with at least biochemi-
cal progression had developed in 287 patients
assigned to ADT alone; 137 patients of these
patients had received docetaxel for castration-
resistant prostate cancer, and another 10 patients
had received docetaxel before castration resis-
tance was confirmed. In addition, 104 patients
had received abiraterone or enzalutamide after
confirmed castration resistance (9 of these pa-
tients may have received placebo as part of a
trial). Table S3 in the Supplementary Appendix
documents the use of other therapies that have
been shown to prolong overall survival in patients
with castration-resistant prostate cancer.



DISCUSSION
Improvements in outcomes in men with meta-
static castration-resistant prostate cancer have
been achieved with the use of cytotoxic chemo-
therapy, next-generation hormonal therapies,
immunotherapy, and therapy with radiopharma-
ceutical agents. 19-25 This study showed that



744
